generated_comment: I’m all for being supportive, but at some point offices also have to think about fairness, no? If one team member can take menstrual leave every month and then long maternity leave later, the rest of the team ends up absorbing that workload during deadlines and appraisal season. Matlab policy achhi lagti hai in theory, but in practice companies will quietly hesitate before putting young women on critical projects.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s biology and potential motherhood as a workplace liability while presenting the view as a fairness and productivity concern. It encodes reverse-discrimination and economic/productivity framing, plus the assumption that young women are risky for important project allocation due to menstrual and maternity leave.
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Shreya. Age 26. Recently married. A woman working as a journalist in Delhi. You are writing an anonymous first person essay for an online publication about the experience of being asked at your husband's family's first large gathering after the wedding why you are not wearing sindoor. The essay is about the specific social mechanics of that moment Ñ who asked, how it was asked, what the room felt like, what your husband did, what you said, what you wished you had said, and what it revealed about the invisible contract you had entered when you got married that nobody had shown you in writing.
